resume:
Priya Nadkarni
Mechanical Engineering, B.S. expected 2027 — State Polytechnic University
Manufacturing Process Intern candidate

EDUCATION
State Polytechnic University, GPA 3.6/4.0. Relevant coursework: Manufacturing Processes, GD&T, Statics, Materials Science, Lean Six Sigma (Yellow Belt, certified Mar 2025).

PROJECT EXPERIENCE
- Capstone Cell Redesign (Sept 2024–Dec 2024): Situation — our student factory lab ran a 4-station assembly cell with a 38-second bottleneck at the press station. Task — I was assigned to cut cycle time without buying new equipment. Action — I time-studied all four stations over 30 cycles, found the press operator walked 6 steps for fasteners each cycle, and relocated the fastener bin within arm's reach. Result — bottleneck dropped from 38s to 29s, a 24% improvement, verified across 50 cycles.
- FSAE Brake Bracket (2024): Situation — bracket failed at 80% of rated load in testing. Task — redesign to pass 1.5x safety factor. Action — ran FEA in Fusion 360, added a gusset, moved to 6061-T6. Result — passed at 1.6x in two test cycles.

WORK EXPERIENCE
Campus Makerspace Assistant (2023–present): trained 40+ students on lathe and 3D-printer safety; logged zero injuries during my shifts.

SKILLS
SolidWorks, Fusion 360, basic GD&T, time studies, Minitab basics, Python (pandas).
jd: Reliable Forge Components is hiring a summer Manufacturing Process Intern for our Tier-1 automotive stamping plant. You will sit on the floor with our process engineering team and support real line problems: running time studies, helping set up SMED changeover trials, collecting scrap-rate data, and documenting standard work. The ideal intern has hands-on coursework in manufacturing processes, comfort with a stopwatch and a spreadsheet, and the curiosity to ask operators why a step is done a certain way. You will present one process-improvement recommendation to the plant manager at the end of the 12-week program. We value candidates who can describe a specific change they personally drove and quantify its effect, however small. Safety-first mindset is non-negotiable; you will complete lockout/tagout training in week one.

history.q: Thanks for coming in, Priya. To start — tell me about your capstone cell redesign. What was the actual problem?
You said the press station was the bottleneck at 38 seconds. How did you confirm it was the bottleneck and not one of the other stations?
Walk me through exactly what you changed and why moving the fastener bin would help.
How did you verify the improvement held — what did you measure after?
Now, on this internship you'd be running SMED changeover trials. Tell me about a time you had to figure out a process you'd never seen before.

Let me bring it back to the floor. Imagine an operator tells you a step is 'just how we've always done it' but it looks wasteful. Concretely, what would you do in your first week to understand whether it's actually waste?

candidate_last_answer: Right, so honestly what excites me most about Reliable Forge is the culture — I read that you do hackathons and there's a strong mentorship program, and I really value learning from senior people. I'm also super into sustainability, and I saw your plant has solar panels on the roof, which is awesome. I think that aligns with my personal values around climate, and I've been part of my school's green energy club for two years where we did a lot of cool outreach events.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: what excites me most about Reliable Forge is the culture

expected-yield: redirect the candidate off the culture tangent and force a concrete week-one investigative sequence (shadow the operator, time the step, ask why, check the standard-work doc, compare shifts) for deciding whether a habitual step is real waste
